summary: Drew DeVault shares that he has reached burnout after a relentless series of personal crises throughout 2023. Starting with a theft on New Year's Eve, he faced escalating difficulties including a breakup, friends in crisis, gossip, and ultimately a death by suicide in his social circle. Despite trying to pace himself and allow productivity to dip without guilt, he reached a breaking point where for the first time he felt intimidated by the work ahead rather than ready to dig in. He announces he is stepping back to rest, with SourceHut colleagues and Hare maintainers covering his responsibilities.
key_points: A cascade of personal crises in 2023 — theft, breakup, friends in distress, public gossip, and a death — compounded into burnout
DeVault's identity as the dependable, always-available friend became unsustainable when his own support network was stretched thin by the same crises
His lifelong resilience mindset of 'give up or get to work' failed him for the first time, marking a qualitative shift in his mental state
He emphasizes having already delegated responsibility — SourceHut colleagues and formal Hare maintainers — so his projects can continue without him
The post is unusually personal for someone who keeps private and public lives carefully separated, signaling the severity of the moment
money_quotes: One day, sitting at my workstation, I stopped typing, stared blankly at the screen for a few seconds, and a switch flipped in my head.

Dependable Drew, an ear to listen, a shoulder to cry on, always knowing the right words to say, ready to help and proud to be there for his friends. Friends who, amidst these crises, are struggling to be there for him.
A selfish end for a selfish person, a person who had hurt people I love; a final, cruel cut to the wounds we were trying to heal.
When you're staring down a hard problem, one which might take years or even decades to finish, you have two choices: give up or get to work. The years are going to pass either way.

I am used to finding myself at the base of a mountain, picking up my shovel, and getting started. Equipped with this mindset, I have patiently ground down more than one mountain in my time. But this week, for the first time in my life, as I gazed upon that mountain, I felt intimidated.
I allowed my productivity to wane as necessary, unburdened by guilt in so doing.
key_insight: Even the most resilient builders can reach a breaking point when personal crises compound faster than they can recover, and the healthiest response is to step back and let the systems you built carry on without you.
